name: org.machanism.machai:machai-mcp-server
description: Machai MCP Server is a Java 17 Maven library and executable server component that provides the Model Context Protocol (MCP) server layer for the Machai AI framework. It is designed for exposing GenAI tools through MCP-compatible transports so that AI clients and orchestration runtimes can discover and invoke tools over standard protocols. The library focuses on server-side MCP runtime concerns such as tool registration, transport setup, startup lifecycle, project directory configuration, and bridging existing Machai GenAI tool definitions into MCP tool specifications. The artifact includes abstractions for common MCP server behavior, HTTP-specific server support, and concrete server implementations for stateless HTTP, streamable HTTP, and STDIO transports. It also provides an adapter for transforming Machai tool metadata and handlers into MCP-compatible tool specifications and a command-line entry point for starting the server in the desired mode. This artifact is most relevant for developers building AI integration servers, local agent tooling, IDE assistants, automation backends, and protocol adapters that need to expose Java-based GenAI tools over MCP. It belongs to the adapter and infrastructure layers because it bridges application tools into the MCP ecosystem and hosts runnable transport endpoints for external AI clients.

usageContext: Expose Machai GenAI tools to MCP-compatible clients
Run local or remote AI tool servers over STDIO or HTTP
Bridge existing Java tool functions into MCP tool specifications
Host protocol endpoints for agent runtimes, IDE assistants, and automation workflows
Build custom MCP servers with reusable transport and startup abstractions

constructors.description: Creates an HTTP MCP server configured for stateless synchronous request handling. Use this constructor when you want to expose Machai GenAI tools over HTTP and then configure runtime details such as port, project directory, tool registration, and startup lifecycle through the inherited server API.

examples.description: Creates a stateless HTTP MCP server, points it at the current project directory, configures port 8080, registers available tools, and starts listening for MCP requests. This is a practical how-to-use scenario for hosting an MCP endpoint behind a local development server or internal automation service.

constructors.description: Creates an HTTP MCP server variant for streamable synchronous MCP interactions. Use this constructor when you need HTTP transport with streamable request processing semantics while still relying on the same shared server abstraction and tool registration model.

examples.description: Creates a streamable HTTP MCP server, assigns the project directory, binds it to port 8081, loads its tools, and starts the runtime. This example demonstrates a real-world setup for serving MCP requests to clients that expect HTTP-based access with streamable server behavior.

constructors.description: Creates an MCP server that communicates over standard input and standard output. This is the preferred constructor for local tool integrations where an MCP client launches the Java process directly and exchanges protocol messages through STDIO instead of opening an HTTP port.

examples.description: Creates a STDIO MCP server for process-based integrations, assigns the project directory, registers tools, and starts the MCP runtime. This is a practical how-to-use scenario for editor plugins, desktop assistants, or local agents that launch the server as a subprocess.

constructors.description: Creates a generic adapter that turns Machai GenAI tool definitions into MCP tool specifications using a supplied specification list and builder strategy. Use it when you are composing your own server flow or need direct control over how tool functions are translated into MCP-compatible handlers.

examples.description: Shows the structural setup for creating a GenericGenaiAdapter with a mutable specification list and a tool specification builder. In a real integration, the builder would convert tool metadata and call handlers into the concrete MCP specification type required by the chosen transport implementation.

features.name: tools
features.description: Loads and registers GenAI tools with the stateless HTTP MCP server. This is the step that prepares the server's MCP-exposed capabilities before startup, making it essential for publishing tool functionality to remote clients.

examples.description: Registers the server's available tools before starting the HTTP transport. This is a practical preparation step whenever you are assembling an MCP server that should expose tool calls to external clients.

features.name: start
features.description: Starts the HTTP server and listens for incoming MCP requests on the configured port. Use this feature after configuring the server and registering tools to make the MCP endpoint available to clients.

examples.description: Runs a stateless HTTP MCP endpoint on port 8080 after tool registration. This how-to-use example matches a common real-world deployment scenario for internal AI tool servers or local development endpoints.

features.name: start
features.description: Builds and returns the configured MCP runtime for STDIO-based operation. This feature enables process-driven MCP integrations where an external tool launches the server and communicates through standard streams.

examples.description: Starts the STDIO server after tool registration, which is a typical setup for local MCP integrations used by editors, command wrappers, or desktop AI assistants.

features.name: setPort
features.description: Configures the HTTP listening port for MCP server implementations based on the HTTP server abstraction. This is a primary runtime configuration step for exposing the server on a known endpoint.

examples.description: Assigns a custom port to a streamable HTTP MCP server before startup. This is useful when integrating with existing service topologies, reverse proxies, or local development environments.

features.name: setProjectDir
features.description: Sets the project directory used by the MCP server runtime. This feature is important when the server must resolve project-scoped resources, configuration, or tool execution context from a specific filesystem location.

examples.description: Points the MCP server at a specific project directory before startup. This is a practical setup step when the exposed tools need to inspect or operate on a target project chosen by the user or launching process.

features.name: addTool
features.signature: void org.machanism.machai.mcp.server.GenericGenaiAdapter.addTool(java.lang.String name, java.lang.String description, org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.ToolFunction toolFunction, org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.ParamDescriptor... paramDescriptors)
features.description: Registers a new tool with the generic adapter, allowing a Machai tool function and its parameter metadata to be converted into an MCP-exposed specification. This is one of the key bridging capabilities of the artifact.

examples.description: Demonstrates the usage pattern for registering a tool with the adapter. In a production setup, the ToolFunction and ParamDescriptor values would come from your Machai tool implementation, allowing the adapter to expose that tool through MCP.

features.name: main
features.description: Provides the command-line entry point for launching the MCP server application. This is the main executable feature for using the artifact as a ready-to-run component rather than only as an embedded library.

examples.description: Runs the MCP server from the command line using the main entry point. This example shows the basic invocation pattern for a standalone runtime scenario where the server is launched as a Java process by an operator, script, or external MCP client.

Step-by-step how-to-use scenario for the ready-to-run component. First install the library in a Maven-based application by adding the dependency. Then build or resolve dependencies so the runtime classpath contains the server artifact and its transitive libraries. Finally launch the McpServer main class to start the MCP server process in a real-world local integration workflow.

customizations.name: AbstractMcpServer.ToolSpecificationBuilder
customizations.description: Strategy interface used to build transport-specific MCP tool specification objects from generic tool definitions and call handlers. Developers can provide implementations that adapt the same logical tool model to different server exchanges or transport runtimes.

examples.description: Shows the customization point used by GenericGenaiAdapter and transport-specific builders. In a concrete integration, developers provide an implementation that knows how to create the MCP tool specification object required by their server runtime.

customizations.name: setTransportProvider
customizations.description: Allows overriding the HTTP servlet transport provider used by the abstract HTTP MCP server. This is a configuration extension point for customizing HTTP request handling infrastructure.

examples.description: Demonstrates the server customization pattern for swapping in a specific HTTP servlet transport provider before startup. This is useful when integrating the MCP server into an existing servlet environment or custom request-processing stack.

studs.name: org.machanism.machai.mcp.server.AbstractMcpServer.ToolSpecificationBuilder
studs.description: Interface for building tool and tool specification objects for MCP servers. It acts as the transport abstraction point for converting generic tool metadata into concrete specification instances bound to a specific server exchange type.

examples.description: Represents the extension contract used by the server infrastructure to produce transport-specific tool specifications. Implement this interface when creating a new MCP transport or adapting the library to another exchange model.

studs.name: org.machanism.machai.mcp.server.AbstractMcpServer
studs.description: Abstract base class for MCP server implementations. It centralizes shared metadata, startup lifecycle hooks, tool registration entry points, and project directory handling for concrete STDIO and HTTP servers.

examples.description: Represents the common superclass that developers extend when introducing a new transport-specific MCP server implementation while reusing the common lifecycle and tool registration model.

examples.title: Embed a stateless HTTP MCP server in a Java application
examples.scenario: How to use the library to expose Machai tools over HTTP for remote MCP clients.
steps: Add the Maven dependency for org.machanism.machai:machai-mcp-server:1.2.0 to your project.
Create an org.machanism.machai.mcp.server.HttpStatelessMcpServer instance with your server name and version.
Set the project directory if your tools need project-scoped context or file access.
Set the HTTP port, call tools() to register the available tools, and invoke start() to begin serving requests.
examples.description: This end-to-end example shows the standard embedding pattern for publishing a Machai-backed MCP endpoint over HTTP. It is suitable for backend services, internal AI gateways, or development servers that need network-based MCP access.

examples.title: Run the MCP server as a STDIO subprocess
examples.scenario: How to use the ready-to-run server component from a local MCP client or automation tool.
steps: Install the component by adding the Maven dependency or by obtaining the built JAR on the Java classpath.
Ensure a Java 17 runtime is available in the execution environment.
Launch org.machanism.machai.mcp.server.McpServer or create a org.machanism.machai.mcp.server.StdioMcpServer instance in your own bootstrap code.
Communicate with the process over standard input and output using the Model Context Protocol.
examples.description: This practical how-to-use scenario demonstrates using the artifact as a subprocess-based MCP server for local tools such as IDE extensions, local agent runners, or desktop AI assistants that prefer STDIO transport.

examples.title: Register custom Machai tools through the generic adapter
examples.scenario: How to bridge existing Machai tool definitions into MCP tool specifications.
steps: Create a mutable list that will hold the generated transport-specific MCP tool specifications.
Provide an implementation of org.machanism.machai.mcp.server.AbstractMcpServer.ToolSpecificationBuilder for your chosen exchange and specification types.
Construct org.machanism.machai.mcp.server.GenericGenaiAdapter with the list and builder.
Call addTool(...) for each Machai tool you want to expose through MCP.
examples.description: This example focuses on the core adapter use case of the library: transforming Machai tool functions into MCP-discoverable tool specifications. It is useful when building custom server compositions or adding new transport implementations.
